figures.caption: Fig. 5: GreenGNN energy profiling pipeline. The Profiler Controller reads partition metadata 1⃝, dispatches controlled fetches 2⃝– 3⃝, collects transfer metadata 4⃝, and triggers energy measurement 5⃝– 6⃝. The Statistical Analyzer derives perowner energy and latency coefficients from the raw readings.
Fig. 1: Energy breakdown of distributed GraphSAGE training on the OGBN-Products dataset.

Fig. 6: GPU vs. CPU energy breakdown for Reddit at B=2000 across window sizes. The dashed line marks the DGL-METIS baseline (128.4 kJ). Both components decrease as W increases, with CPU energy accounting for most of the reduction.

Fig. 7: Total energy consumption (GPU + CPU, all nodes) across three datasets at B=2000. GreenGNN achieves the lowest energy in all cases. Annotations show percentage reduction relative to Default DGL. GraphStorm on Papers100M is truncated (actual: 1,452 kJ).
TABLE III: Simulator ranking fidelity. For each window size, the simulator’s predicted rank (S) and measured rank (A) are shown. ✓ marks correct top-1 predictions. τ is Kendall’s rank correlation.
TABLE II: Comprehensive energy and epoch time across all frameworks, datasets, and batch sizes. Bold = best (lowest) per column. ∆ = reduction vs. Default DGL. All energy values in kJ.
Fig. 2: Initiation vs. payload energy as a function of transfer size. The dashed line marks the crossover (≈965 nodes) between the initiation-dominated and payload-dominated regimes.
Fig. 8: GPU energy at B=2000. GreenGNN achieves 37– 71% GPU energy reduction over Default DGL. GraphStorm on Papers100M is truncated (actual: 340 kJ).
Fig. 9: Energy vs. epoch time on OGBN-Papers100M at B=2000 (log-scale y-axis). GreenGNN is Pareto-optimal. The shaded region denotes the Pareto-dominated area.
TABLE I: Comparison of distributed GNN systems along key design axes.

Fig. 3: GreenGNN architecture overview. Circled numbers indicate execution order. Steps 1⃝– 8⃝ constitute the offline phase (trace collection, energy profiling, and window selection); steps 9⃝–10⃝ execute at runtime (hot-set caching and feature fetching).
Fig. 11: GreenGNN’s reduction over Default DGL (%) across three metrics and three batch sizes per dataset. All cells are positive. GPU energy reductions (36–71%) are uniformly the largest.
Fig. 10: Epoch-time speedup relative to Default DGL at B=2000. The dashed line marks the DGL baseline (1.0×).
